schools.summary: The daughters of Zelophehad coming with their claim: 'then came the daughters of Zelophehad, the son of Hepher, the son of Gilead, the son of Machir, the son of Manasseh, of the families of Manasseh the son of Joseph: and these are the names of his daughters; Mahlah, Noah, and Hoglah, and Milcah, and Tirzah' — the five daughters of Zelophehad, of the tribe of Manasseh, came forward with a claim concerning their father's inheritance (the the daughters of Zelophehad, the petitioners, a man who left no sons, the of the families of Manasseh the son of Joseph, their tribe and descent, the these are the names of his daughters; Mahlah, Noah, and Hoglah, and Milcah, and Tirzah, the five named, the figure, the daughters coming forward in faith and order to seek their inheritance, the truth that even the women of Israel had a claim to the land of promise, and sought it, the figure of those who lay hold of the inheritance, the daughters honored for their faith in the promise, the names recorded for a memorial, their cause heard by God, vv.7); the daughters of Zelophehad of Manasseh coming with their claim (the petitioners) — the petitioners (the daughters); 'Mahlah, Noah, and Hoglah, and Milcah, and Tirzah' (the five daughters named, recorded for a memorial, the figure, those who lay hold of the inheritance, the providence, the daughters coming, the claim stated to follow, vv.2-4) — Henry, Gill.

schools.summary: The daughters standing before Moses and the assembly: 'and they stood before Moses, and before Eleazar the priest, and before the princes and all the congregation, by the door of the tabernacle of the congregation, saying' — the daughters presented their case openly before Moses, the priest, the princes, and the congregation (the they stood before Moses, and before Eleazar the priest, before the leaders, the and before the princes and all the congregation, before the whole assembly, the by the door of the tabernacle of the congregation, the place of judgment, before God, the figure, the daughters bringing their cause openly and in order before the appointed authorities and before God, the truth that they sought their right in the lawful way, not by force but by petition, the figure of the orderly seeking of justice, the cause brought before God's appointed, the case laid before the LORD at the tabernacle door, vv.5); the daughters standing before the leaders (the lawful appeal) — the appearance (before Moses); 'before the princes and all the congregation, by the door of the tabernacle' (the cause before the whole assembly and God, the place of judgment, the figure, the orderly seeking of justice, the providence, the appearance, the claim stated to follow, vv.3-4) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

schools.summary: The daughters' plea, their father not a rebel but dying without sons: 'our father died in the wilderness, and he was not in the company of them that gathered themselves together against the LORD in the company of Korah; but died in his own sin, and had no sons' — the daughters pleaded that their father died not in rebellion with Korah, but in the common sin of his generation, leaving no sons (the our father died in the wilderness, the father's death among the fallen generation, the he was not in the company... of Korah, not a rebel, not cut off for rebellion, the but died in his own sin, in the common sin of unbelief, not in special rebellion, the and had no sons, the ground of the claim, no male heir, the figure, the careful plea that the father's name not be lost for a sin that did not forfeit the inheritance, the truth that the daughters distinguished their father from the rebels, that his line not be cut off, the figure of the concern for the inheritance and the name, the just distinction between the common sin and the rebellion that forfeits, the daughters' care that their father's portion be preserved, vv.4); the father not a rebel with Korah (the distinction) — the plea (not in rebellion); 'died in his own sin, and had no sons' (the common sin, the lack of male heir, the ground of the claim, the figure, the inheritance not forfeited, the name preserved, the providence, the plea, the request to follow, vv.4) — Calvin, Gill.

themes: Our father died in the wilderness, and he was not in the company of them that gathered themselves together against the LORD in the company of Korah; but died in his own sin, and had no sons
the daughters' plea, their father not a rebel but dying without sons
the daughters distinguishing their father from the rebels, that his line not be cut off
the inheritance not forfeited by the common sin

schools.summary: The daughters' request for a possession to preserve their father's name: 'why should the name of our father be done away from among his family, because he hath no son? Give unto us therefore a possession among the brethren of our father' — the daughters asked that they be given an inheritance, that their father's name not perish for want of a son (the why should the name of our father be done away... because he hath no son?, the plea against the loss of the name, the Give unto us therefore a possession among the brethren of our father, the request for the inheritance, the figure, the request for the inheritance, that the name and portion be preserved, the truth that the concern for the name in Israel was the concern for the inheritance in the land of promise, the figure of those who seek their portion in the inheritance, the daughters' faith and care, the just petition that God would honor, that the name be not lost, vv.7); the plea against the loss of the father's name (the concern for the name) — the plea (the name preserved); 'Give unto us therefore a possession among the brethren of our father' (the request for the inheritance, the portion sought, the figure, those who seek their portion in the inheritance, the providence, the request, Moses bringing the cause to God to follow, vv.5) — Wesley, Gill.

themes: Why should the name of our father be done away from among his family, because he hath no son? Give unto us therefore a possession among the brethren of our father

schools.summary: Moses bringing their cause before the LORD: 'and Moses brought their cause before the LORD' — Moses, not deciding of himself, laid the daughters' case before God for his judgment (the Moses brought their cause before the LORD, the cause referred to God, the figure, the humility and faithfulness of Moses, not presuming to judge a new case but seeking God's word, the truth that the leader of God's people sought direction from God in what was not yet revealed, the figure of the cause committed to God, the matter referred to his judgment, the model of seeking God's will rather than one's own, 'if any of you lack wisdom, let him ask of God,' James 1:5, the just cause brought before the righteous Judge); Moses referring the cause to God (not deciding himself) — the referral (the cause brought); 'before the LORD' (the cause referred to God's judgment, James 1:5, the figure, the cause committed to God, the providence, the appeal, God's answer to follow, vv.6-7) — Calvin, Gill.

themes: And Moses brought their cause before the LORD
Moses bringing their cause before the LORD
Moses not presuming to judge a new case but seeking God's word, the cause referred to the righteous Judge

schools.summary: The LORD answering Moses concerning the daughters' claim: 'and the LORD spake unto Moses, saying' — God answered Moses, now to give his judgment on the daughters' cause (vv.7, the speaking, God's answer, the judgment given, the occasion, the cause referred to God, now his decision, that the daughters' claim was just, the figure, God answering the cause brought to him, giving his righteous judgment, the truth that God hears and answers the cause committed to him, the just claim granted, the figure of the answer of God to those who seek his will, the matter decided by his word, the LORD giving the law of inheritance through this case, vv.7-11, God answering, showing he hears the cause referred to him and gives righteous judgment); the LORD answering Moses (the judgment) — the answer (God's word); the LORD spake unto Moses — the answer to the cause (the providence, God giving his righteous judgment) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

schools.summary: God's judgment that the daughters spoke right and should inherit: 'the daughters of Zelophehad speak right: thou shalt surely give them a possession of an inheritance among their father's brethren; and thou shalt cause the inheritance of their father to pass unto them' — the just grant — 'the daughters of Zelophehad speak right: thou shalt surely give them a possession of an inheritance among their father's brethren': God declared the daughters' claim just and commanded that their father's inheritance pass to them (the witnesses noting the significance — the equity of God's judgment, owning the daughters' right, God himself approving their cause, 'they speak right,' and securing their portion, the lesson — the justice and tenderness of God's law, providing for the daughters that the name and inheritance be not lost, the law of inheritance established through their case, that where there is no son, the daughters inherit, vv.8, the truth that God's law cares for the weak and the unprovided, securing the right of the fatherless daughters, the figure — the inheritance secured to those who seek it in faith, the daughters granted their portion in the land of promise, the equity of God who judges righteously and gives to each their due, 'shall not the Judge of all the earth do right?' Genesis 18:25, the figure of the inheritance given to the faithful, even the daughters, a token of the wideness of God's grace that makes heirs of those who seemed to have no portion, 'if children, then heirs,' Romans 8:17; Galatians 3:28-29, the truth that in the inheritance of God there is provision for all his people, the figure of the just inheritance secured by the equity of God's law); God owning the daughters' right, 'they speak right' (the equity, the cause just, Genesis 18:25) — the approval (the cause owned); the daughters granted their father's inheritance, the portion secured (the just provision, the inheritance passing to them, Romans 8:17; Galatians 3:28-29) — the figure (the inheritance secured to the faithful, the providence, the grant, the law of inheritance to follow, vv.8-11) — Calvin, Ellicott, Clarke, Gill.

debates.question: What does God's granting of the daughters' claim establish?

sides.position: The equity and tenderness of God's law, owning the daughters' just right ('they speak right') and securing their portion that the name and inheritance be not lost - a figure of the equity of God who judges righteously (Genesis 18:25) and of the inheritance given to the faithful, making heirs of those who seemed to have no portion (Romans 8:17; Galatians 3:28-29)

themes: The daughters of Zelophehad speak right: thou shalt surely give them a possession of an inheritance among their father's brethren; and thou shalt cause the inheritance of their father to pass unto them
God's judgment that the daughters spoke right and should inherit (the crux of the just inheritance and the equity of God's law)
God owning the daughters' just claim and securing their portion, the justice and tenderness of his law
a figure of the equity of God who judges righteously and the inheritance given to the faithful (Genesis 18:25; Romans 8:17; Galatians 3:28-29)

schools.summary: The law that the daughter inherits where there is no son: 'and thou shalt speak unto the children of Israel, saying, If a man die, and have no son, then ye shall cause his inheritance to pass unto his daughter' — from the daughters' case God gave a general law: where there is no son, the daughter inherits (the if a man die, and have no son, the case of no male heir, the then ye shall cause his inheritance to pass unto his daughter, the daughter the heir, the figure, the general law of inheritance established from the particular case, the truth that God's law provided for the daughters, that the inheritance not be lost, the figure of the order of inheritance, the lasting statute drawn from the just cause, the equity of the law that secures the portion to the next of kin, beginning with the daughter, the provision for all in the inheritance of God's people, vv.9-11); the case of no son (the law's occasion) — the law (no male heir); 'ye shall cause his inheritance to pass unto his daughter' (the daughter the heir, the lasting statute, the figure, the order of inheritance established, the providence, the law, the further order to follow, vv.9-11) — Ellicott, Gill.

themes: And thou shalt speak unto the children of Israel, saying, If a man die, and have no son, then ye shall cause his inheritance to pass unto his daughter
the law that the daughter inherits where there is no son
the general statute drawn from the daughters' just case, the daughter the heir
that the inheritance be not lost, the lasting provision of God's law

schools.summary: The law that the brethren inherit where there is no daughter: 'and if he have no daughter, then ye shall give his inheritance unto his brethren' — the law continues the order: where there is no daughter, the brethren inherit (the if he have no daughter, the next case, the then ye shall give his inheritance unto his brethren, the brethren the heirs, the figure, the order of inheritance descending to the next of kin, the truth that God's law provided for every case, the inheritance always preserved in the family, the figure of the ordered succession, the portion kept in the kindred, the care of God's law that the inheritance be not lost but pass to the nearest, vv.10-11); the case of no daughter (the next in order) — the case (no daughter); 'ye shall give his inheritance unto his brethren' (the brethren the heirs, the ordered succession, the figure, the portion kept in the kindred, the providence, the order, the uncles and kinsmen to follow, vv.10-11) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

themes: And if he have no daughter, then ye shall give his inheritance unto his brethren
the law that the brethren inherit where there is no daughter
the inheritance descending to the next of kin, the portion kept in the family
the care of God's law that it be not lost

schools.summary: The law that the father's brethren inherit where there are no brethren: 'and if he have no brethren, then ye shall give his inheritance unto his father's brethren' — the order continues: where there are no brethren, the father's brethren (uncles) inherit (the if he have no brethren, the next case, the then ye shall give his inheritance unto his father's brethren, the uncles the heirs, the figure, the order of inheritance still descending to the nearest kin, the truth that the inheritance was kept within the family at every remove, the figure of the ordered succession, the portion never lost from the kindred, the care of God's law to preserve the inheritance, vv.11); the case of no brethren (the next in order) — the case (no brethren); 'ye shall give his inheritance unto his father's brethren' (the uncles the heirs, the ordered succession, the figure, the portion kept in the kindred, the providence, the order, the nearest kinsman to follow, vv.11) — Wesley, Gill.

themes: And if he have no brethren, then ye shall give his inheritance unto his father's brethren
the law that the uncles inherit where there are no brethren
the inheritance descending to the uncles, the portion kept in the kindred at every remove
the care of God's law

schools.summary: The law completed, the nearest kinsman inheriting, a lasting statute: 'and if his father have no brethren, then ye shall give his inheritance unto his kinsman that is next to him of his family, and he shall possess it: and it shall be unto the children of Israel a statute of judgment, as the LORD commanded Moses' — the order is completed: failing all nearer, the next kinsman inherits, and this is a lasting statute (the if his father have no brethren, the last case, the ye shall give his inheritance unto his kinsman that is next to him, the nearest kinsman the heir, the it shall be unto the children of Israel a statute of judgment, the lasting law, the figure, the inheritance always kept in the family, passing to the nearest kin, the truth that God's law made full provision, the portion never lost, the figure of the ordered inheritance, the statute of judgment, the just and lasting law, the kinsman-redeemer who keeps the inheritance, a figure of the near kinsman who secures the portion, Ruth 4:4-6); the nearest kinsman the heir (the inheritance kept, Ruth 4:4) — the kinsman (the nearest heir); 'a statute of judgment, as the LORD commanded Moses' (the lasting law, the statute of judgment, the figure, the just and lasting order of inheritance, the providence, the statute, Moses bid to view the land to follow, vv.12-14) — Wesley, Gill.

themes: And if his father have no brethren, then ye shall give his inheritance unto his kinsman that is next to him of his family, and he shall possess it: and it shall be unto the children of Israel a statute of judgment, as the LORD commanded Moses
the law completed, the nearest kinsman inheriting, a lasting statute
the inheritance passing to the nearest kin, never lost from the family, the lasting statute of God's just law

schools.summary: Moses bid to view the land from Mount Abarim: 'and the LORD said unto Moses, Get thee up into this mount Abarim, and see the land which I have given unto the children of Israel' — God bid Moses ascend Abarim to see, but not enter, the promised land (the Get thee up into this mount Abarim, the ascent to the height, the and see the land which I have given unto the children of Israel, the land seen but not entered, the figure, Moses given to see the land from afar, the inheritance he might behold but not possess, the truth that Moses, for the sin at Meribah, would see the land but not enter it, vv.14, the figure of the law in Moses that brings to the border but cannot bring in, the land entered only by Joshua, Jesus, the tender mercy that let Moses see the land, 'the LORD shewed him all the land,' Deuteronomy 34:1, the inheritance given to Israel, beheld by the dying servant); Moses bid to ascend Abarim (the command to view) — the ascent (to the mount); 'see the land which I have given unto the children of Israel' (the land seen but not entered, Deuteronomy 34:1, the figure, the law that brings to the border but cannot bring in, the providence, the command, Moses told of his death to follow, vv.13) — Henry, Gill.

themes: And the LORD said unto Moses, Get thee up into this mount Abarim, and see the land which I have given unto the children of Israel

schools.summary: Moses told he would be gathered to his people, as Aaron: 'and when thou hast seen it, thou also shalt be gathered unto thy people, as Aaron thy brother was gathered' — the gathering — 'thou also shalt be gathered unto thy people, as Aaron thy brother was gathered': Moses, having seen the land, would die and be gathered to his people, as Aaron had been (the witnesses noting the significance — the death of Moses, gathered to his people like Aaron, the servant who, for the sin at Meribah, would die outside the land, beholding but not entering, the lesson — the solemn truth that even the greatest servant of God dies under the sentence of his fault, yet is gathered to his people, the phrase 'gathered unto thy people' speaking of more than burial, the gathering to the company of the departed faithful, the hope beyond death, the tenderness in the judgment — Moses dies as Aaron did, gathered to his people, not cast off, the figure — the law in Moses cannot bring into the rest, but dies at the border, while Joshua, Jesus, leads in, the truth that the servant who saw the land from afar is gathered to his people, 'these all died in faith, not having received the promises, but having seen them afar off,' Hebrews 11:13, the figure of the death of the faithful, gathered to their people in hope, the rest of the inheritance entered only through the true Joshua, the gathering to the people of God beyond death, the hope that outlives the sentence, the figure of the servant gathered to his people, who saw the promise but entered through another); Moses told he would die, gathered to his people after seeing the land (the death of the servant, the gathering, Hebrews 11:13) — the death (gathered to his people); Moses gathered as Aaron, dying outside the land for the sin at Meribah, yet gathered to his people in hope (the tenderness in the judgment, the gathering beyond death) — the figure (the faithful gathered to their people, the rest entered through the true Joshua, the providence, the sentence, the cause at Meribah to follow, vv.14) — Calvin, Keil, Ellicott, Wesley, Gill.

debates.question: What does Moses being 'gathered unto thy people' signify?

sides.position: The death of the servant who saw the land from afar but did not enter (for the sin at Meribah), gathered to his people in hope - the phrase speaking of the company of the departed faithful beyond death (Hebrews 11:13), a figure of the law that cannot bring into the rest but dies at the border, the rest entered only through the true Joshua

themes: And when thou hast seen it, thou also shalt be gathered unto thy people, as Aaron thy brother was gathered
Moses told he would be gathered to his people, as Aaron (the crux of the death of the servant who saw but did not enter)
Moses dying outside the land for the sin at Meribah, beholding the inheritance but not entering, yet gathered to his people in hope
a figure of the law that cannot bring into the rest, the rest entered only through the true Joshua (Hebrews 11:13)

schools.summary: The cause of the sentence, the sin at Meribah: 'for ye rebelled against my commandment in the desert of Zin, in the strife of the congregation, to sanctify me at the water before their eyes: that is the water of Meribah in Kadesh in the wilderness of Zin' — the reason Moses would not enter: the sin at Meribah, where he failed to sanctify God before the people (the ye rebelled against my commandment in the desert of Zin, the disobedience at Zin, the in the strife of the congregation, at the waters of strife, the to sanctify me at the water before their eyes, the failure to honor God before the people, Numbers 20:12, the that is the water of Meribah, the place named, the figure, the sin that barred Moses from the land, the failure to sanctify God, the truth that even the greatest servant is judged for his fault, the sin remembered, the figure of the seriousness of failing to honor God before the people, the sin that kept Moses from the inheritance, the solemn warning that God is sanctified in them that come near him, Leviticus 10:3); the sin at Zin, the strife of the congregation (the disobedience, Numbers 20:12) — the rebellion (at Meribah); 'to sanctify me at the water before their eyes' (the failure to honor God before the people, Leviticus 10:3, the figure, the sin that barred Moses, the providence, the cause, Moses's prayer for a successor to follow, vv.15-17) — Clarke, Gill.

themes: For ye rebelled against my commandment in the desert of Zin, in the strife of the congregation, to sanctify me at the water before their eyes: that is the water of Meribah in Kadesh in the wilderness of Zin
the cause of the sentence, the sin at Meribah
the sin at the waters of strife, the failure to honor God before the people
the fault that barred even Moses from the inheritance (Numbers 20:12; Leviticus 10:3)

schools.summary: Moses turning to pray for a successor: 'and Moses spake unto the LORD, saying' — Moses, hearing of his death, turned not to himself but to pray for the people's good, that a successor be appointed (vv.16-17, the Moses spake unto the LORD, the prayer, the figure, the selflessness of Moses, who, told of his death, thought first of the people, the truth that the faithful shepherd cares more for the flock than for himself, praying for their good, the figure of the servant's love for God's people, seeking their welfare even in his own departure, the prayer that the congregation not be left without a shepherd, vv.17, Moses praying for a successor, showing his care for the flock above himself); Moses praying for the people (the selfless prayer) — the prayer (Moses speaking); 'unto the LORD' (the appeal to God for a successor, the care for the flock, the figure, the shepherd's love for the people, the providence, the prayer, the request for a shepherd to follow, vv.16-17) — Henry, Gill.

themes: And Moses spake unto the LORD, saying
Moses turning to pray for a successor
Moses, told of his death, praying not for himself but for the people's good
the shepherd's care for the flock above himself

schools.summary: Moses's prayer that God set a man over the congregation: 'let the LORD, the God of the spirits of all flesh, set a man over the congregation' — Moses asked that God, who knows and gives the spirits of all, appoint a fit leader over the people (the Let the LORD, the God of the spirits of all flesh, God who knows and forms every spirit, the set a man over the congregation, the leader appointed by God, the figure, the appeal to God as the giver of the spirit, that he choose the fit man, the truth that the leader of God's people must be of God's own appointing and gifting, not man's choosing, the figure of the God who knows the hearts and gives the spirit, choosing his servant, the title 'God of the spirits of all flesh' owning that God alone can fit a man for the charge, Numbers 16:22, the prayer that God himself provide the shepherd); God as the giver of the spirit of all (the God who fits the man, Numbers 16:22) — the title (the God of the spirits); 'set a man over the congregation' (the leader appointed by God, the fit man chosen, the figure, the God who gives the spirit choosing his servant, the providence, the petition, the shepherd's work to follow, vv.17) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

themes: Let the LORD, the God of the spirits of all flesh, set a man over the congregation
Moses's prayer that God set a man over the congregation
Moses asking that God, who knows and gives every spirit, appoint a fit leader
the shepherd of God's own choosing and gifting (Numbers 16:22)

schools.summary: Moses's prayer for a shepherd to lead the people, lest they be as sheep without a shepherd: 'which may go out before them, and which may go in before them, and which may lead them out, and which may bring them in; that the congregation of the LORD be not as sheep which have no shepherd' — the shepherd — 'which may go out before them... that the congregation of the LORD be not as sheep which have no shepherd': Moses prayed for a leader who would go before the people in all their goings, that they not be left shepherdless (the witnesses noting the significance — the need of a shepherd to lead the flock of God, the leader who goes out and in before them, in war and peace, in all their ways, that they be guided and not scattered, the lesson — the care of God for his people, that they not be as sheep without a shepherd, the helplessness of the flock without a leader, 'scattered, as sheep having no shepherd,' the pitiable state Moses prayed against, the figure — the great Shepherd of the sheep, fulfilled supremely in Christ, who saw the multitudes 'as sheep not having a shepherd, and was moved with compassion,' Matthew 9:36; Mark 6:34, the true Shepherd who goes before his sheep and leads them out and in, 'he that entereth in by the door is the shepherd of the sheep... he goeth before them, and the sheep follow him,' John 10:2-4, the figure of Joshua the type and Christ the antitype, the Shepherd who leads God's people into the inheritance, the truth that God provides the shepherd for his flock, that they be not scattered, the figure of the good Shepherd who gathers and leads the people of God); the shepherd who goes before the people in all their ways, in and out, leading them (the leader who guides the flock, John 10:4) — the leader (going before); the prayer that God's people not be left shepherdless, scattered as sheep without a shepherd (the need of the shepherd, fulfilled in Christ, Matthew 9:36; Mark 6:34) — the figure (the good Shepherd who leads the flock, the providence, the prayer, the appointment of Joshua to follow, vv.18) — Calvin, Ellicott, Clarke, Wesley, Gill.

debates.question: What does Moses's prayer 'that the congregation be not as sheep which have no shepherd' signify?

sides.position: The need of a shepherd to go before God's flock in all their ways, that they be not scattered - the care of God for his people, a figure of the great Shepherd Christ who saw the multitudes as sheep without a shepherd and was moved with compassion (Matthew 9:36; Mark 6:34), the Shepherd who goes before his sheep and leads them in (John 10:2-4)

themes: Which may go out before them, and which may go in before them, and which may lead them out, and which may bring them in; that the congregation of the LORD be not as sheep which have no shepherd
Moses's prayer for a shepherd to lead the people, lest they be as sheep without a shepherd (the crux of the shepherd over God's flock)
the leader who goes out and in before the people in all their ways, that the flock be not scattered
a figure of the great Shepherd Christ who was moved with compassion for the shepherdless and leads his people in (Matthew 9:36; Mark 6:34; John 10:2-4)

schools.summary: God's appointment of Joshua, the man in whom is the spirit: 'and the LORD said unto Moses, Take thee Joshua the son of Nun, a man in whom is the spirit, and lay thine hand upon him' — the chosen successor — 'Take thee Joshua the son of Nun, a man in whom is the spirit, and lay thine hand upon him': God named Joshua as Moses's successor, a man having the Spirit, to be ordained by the laying on of Moses's hand (the witnesses noting the significance — Joshua chosen as the shepherd Moses prayed for, 'a man in whom is the spirit,' the Spirit of God upon him, fitting him for the charge, the answer to the prayer that God set a man over the congregation, the lesson — that the leader of God's people is one whom God has gifted with his Spirit, the office filled by the Spirit-endued man, the laying on of hands the solemn ordination and the imparting of the charge, the figure — Joshua, whose name is the Hebrew of Jesus, 'the LORD saves,' the type of Christ, the true Joshua, the Spirit-filled Leader who brings God's people into the inheritance, 'upon whom the Spirit of the LORD shall rest,' Isaiah 11:2, the man in whom is the Spirit a figure of Christ anointed with the Spirit without measure, John 3:34, the laying on of hands the figure of the ordination to office and the transmission of authority, Deuteronomy 34:9, the truth that God provides for his people a Spirit-filled shepherd, the type of the great Shepherd, the figure of the chosen successor, the man of the Spirit, who leads the flock into the rest, the figure of the Spirit-filled leader appointed by God, the type of the true Joshua); Joshua chosen, a man having the Spirit of God (the Spirit-endued successor, Isaiah 11:2; John 3:34) — the man (in whom is the spirit); 'lay thine hand upon him' (the laying on of Moses's hand, the solemn ordination and imparting of the charge, the ordination to office, Deuteronomy 34:9, the figure, the transmission of authority, the type of Christ the true Joshua, the providence, the appointment, the public charge before Eleazar to follow, vv.19-23) — Calvin, Gill, JFB, Wesley, Clarke.

debates.question: What does the appointment of Joshua, 'a man in whom is the spirit,' signify?

sides.position: The Spirit-endued shepherd chosen by God as Moses's successor and ordained by the laying on of hands - a figure of Christ the true Joshua (whose name he bears), the Spirit-filled Leader anointed without measure (Isaiah 11:2; John 3:34) who brings God's people into the inheritance, the laying on of hands the figure of ordination and imparting of the Spirit (Deuteronomy 34:9)

themes: And the LORD said unto Moses, Take thee Joshua the son of Nun, a man in whom is the spirit, and lay thine hand upon him
God's appointment of Joshua, the man in whom is the spirit (the crux of the Spirit-filled successor, the type of the true Joshua)
Joshua the Spirit-endued shepherd chosen and ordained by the laying on of hands
a figure of Christ the true Joshua the Spirit-filled Leader who brings God's people into the inheritance (Isaiah 11:2; John 3:34; Deuteronomy 34:9)

schools.summary: Joshua set before the priest and congregation and given a public charge: 'and set him before Eleazar the priest, and before all the congregation; and give him a charge in their sight' — Joshua was to be presented before the priest and the people and solemnly charged in their sight (the set him before Eleazar the priest, and before all the congregation, the public presentation, the and give him a charge in their sight, the solemn commission before all, the figure, the public ordination and charge, that the people might own their new leader, the truth that the leader of God's people is set before them openly and charged before God and the congregation, the figure of the open commissioning of God's servant, the charge given in the sight of all, the orderly transfer of leadership, that all might acknowledge Joshua, vv.20); Joshua presented before the priest and people (the public ordination) — the presentation (before all); 'give him a charge in their sight' (the solemn commission before all, the open charge, the figure, the open commissioning of God's servant, the providence, the charge, the honour put upon Joshua to follow, vv.20) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

themes: And set him before Eleazar the priest, and before all the congregation; and give him a charge in their sight
Joshua set before the priest and congregation and given a public charge
Joshua presented before the priest and people and solemnly commissioned before all
the open commissioning of God's servant, that they might own their new leader

schools.summary: Moses putting some of his honour on Joshua, that the people obey: 'and thou shalt put some of thine honour upon him, that all the congregation of the children of Israel may be obedient' — Moses was to transfer part of his authority and honour to Joshua, that the people might obey him (the thou shalt put some of thine honour upon him, the transfer of authority, the that all the congregation... may be obedient, the end, the people's obedience, the figure, the imparting of authority to the successor, that he be honoured and obeyed, the truth that God establishes his servant in the eyes of the people, putting honour upon him, the 'some of thine honour' — not all, for Moses was unique, but enough for the charge, the figure of the authority given to God's appointed leader, that the people submit, the honour put upon Joshua a token of God's establishing him, that the flock follow the shepherd); Moses transferring part of his honour to Joshua (the authority imparted) — the honour (transferred); 'that all the congregation... may be obedient' (the people's obedience, the end of the honour, the submission to the leader, the figure, the authority that the flock follow the shepherd, the providence, the honour, the counsel of Eleazar and the Urim to follow, vv.21) — Clarke, Gill.

themes: And thou shalt put some of thine honour upon him, that all the congregation of the children of Israel may be obedient
Moses putting some of his honour on Joshua, that the people obey
Moses transferring part of his authority to Joshua, that the people might obey him
the honour put upon God's appointed that the flock follow the shepherd

schools.summary: Joshua to seek counsel through Eleazar and the Urim: 'and he shall stand before Eleazar the priest, who shall ask counsel for him after the judgment of Urim before the LORD: at his word shall they go out, and at his word they shall come in, both he, and all the children of Israel with him, even all the congregation' — Joshua, unlike Moses, would not speak with God face to face, but seek God's will through the priest and the Urim (the he shall stand before Eleazar the priest, who shall ask counsel... after the judgment of Urim, the counsel through the priest and Urim, the at his word shall they go out, and at his word they shall come in, the people guided by God's word so sought, the figure, Joshua subordinate to the word of God sought through the priest, not the immediate revelation Moses had, the truth that the leader and people were to be guided by God's will, sought in the appointed way, the figure of the dependence of God's people on his guidance, the word sought before they go out or in, the contrast with Moses's unique nearness, Numbers 12:8, Joshua led by the Urim, the figure of guidance through God's appointed means, the flock led at God's word); Joshua seeking God's will through the priest and Urim (the appointed guidance) — the counsel (by the Urim); 'at his word shall they go out, and at his word they shall come in' (the people guided by God's word sought through the priest, the dependence on God's guidance, the figure, the flock led at God's word, the providence, the counsel, Moses's obedience in ordaining Joshua to follow, vv.22-23) — Calvin, Gill.

themes: And he shall stand before Eleazar the priest, who shall ask counsel for him after the judgment of Urim before the LORD: at his word shall they go out, and at his word they shall come in
Joshua to seek counsel through Eleazar and the Urim
Joshua and the people guided by God's will sought through the priest and the Urim, the flock led at God's word
the dependence of God's people on his guidance

schools.summary: Moses obeying, setting Joshua before the priest and congregation: 'and Moses did as the LORD commanded him: and he took Joshua, and set him before Eleazar the priest, and before all the congregation' — Moses obeyed at once, presenting Joshua before the priest and the people (the Moses did as the LORD commanded him, the exact obedience, the he took Joshua, and set him before Eleazar the priest, and before all the congregation, the public presentation, the figure, the obedience of Moses, ordaining his own successor without grudging, the truth that the faithful servant obeys God even in appointing the one to take his place, the figure of the selfless obedience of Moses, presenting Joshua before all, the meekness of Moses, who set up his successor as God commanded, the orderly transfer of leadership); Moses obeying exactly (the faithful servant) — the obedience (Moses did so); 'set him before Eleazar the priest, and before all the congregation' (Joshua presented before the priest and people, the public ordination, the figure, the selfless obedience of Moses, the providence, the presentation, the laying on of hands to follow, vv.23) — Wesley, Gill.

themes: And Moses did as the LORD commanded him: and he took Joshua, and set him before Eleazar the priest, and before all the congregation
Moses obeying, setting Joshua before the priest and congregation
Moses presenting his own successor before all as God commanded, without grudging
the faithful servant ordaining the one to take his place

schools.summary: Moses laying his hands on Joshua and giving him the charge: 'and he laid his hands upon him, and gave him a charge, as the LORD commanded by the hand of Moses' — Moses laid his hands on Joshua and solemnly charged him, as God had commanded (the he laid his hands upon him, the laying on of hands, the ordination, the and gave him a charge, the solemn commission, the as the LORD commanded by the hand of Moses, the obedience to God's command, the figure, the ordination of Joshua by the laying on of hands, the imparting of the Spirit and the charge, the truth that Joshua was full of the spirit of wisdom, 'for Moses had laid his hands upon him,' Deuteronomy 34:9, the figure of the ordination to office, the laying on of hands and the charge, the pattern of the solemn setting apart of God's servants, 1 Timothy 4:14, the orderly transfer of leadership completed, Joshua established as Moses's successor, the figure of the imparting of the Spirit and authority for the work of God); Moses laying hands on Joshua and charging him (the ordination, Deuteronomy 34:9) — the ordination (the hands laid on); 'as the LORD commanded by the hand of Moses' (Moses obeying God's command, the ordination as commanded, 1 Timothy 4:14, the figure, the solemn setting apart of God's servant, the providence, the succession secured, before the offerings of ch.28) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

themes: And he laid his hands upon him, and gave him a charge, as the LORD commanded by the hand of Moses
Moses laying his hands on Joshua and giving him the charge
Moses ordaining Joshua by the laying on of hands and the charge, the Spirit and authority imparted
the succession secured, Joshua established as the shepherd of God's people (Deuteronomy 34:9; 1 Timothy 4:14)
